speaker: interviewer
text: Survivor is Jack Rosenthal. OK, please continue. You landed in New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I land from New York--

speaker: interviewer
text: Please tell us briefly how you got to here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I told you about the ladies with the-- with the hot chocolate, and we stayed there. Finally, the bosses came and took us to a home up in the Bronx that was a-- like a-- nuns, for nuns. And we were staying there, and I got a pair of twill. And then I-- Then finally, I was looking-- I moved in with my cousin-- with my cousin in [? Stanton ?] Street. And after a couple of weeks, I had to go out and get a job. And somebody told me that in the-- in the country he made $500 last year. He saved up $500, and that was a lot of money.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, what did you do? So he said that he was a waiter. So I went up to the country, and I wanted to be a waiter, but there was no job for a waiter. So a guy passed by. He was a peddler. And he was peddling, and he said if I could go and help him peddle, and I said yes. He had a little-- a little truck. And he told me at night, write it down in your own language-- I didn't speak English those days-- that I had to say we are selling out play suits, sun suits, shorts, midriffs, halters, all kinds of ladies sportswear in all sizes. We are selling out. So I became a salesman, right? So we would go from bungalow colony to bungalow colony. But the first bungalow I hit I said, we are selling out, and I forgot the rest of the story. So the guy hollered at me, so finally I learned the story, and I became a salesman. And I was in the country there for a couple of months. He paid me $12 a week. Also room and board. And I took care of his garden and everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I came back and I-- I got a steady job in Brooklyn. Triangle Steel, where they made fire doors. Fire doors. And I worked in fire doors. And it was hard work, but it was-- they were paying very good, $1.00 an hour. And I used to put in overtime, $1.50. So I used to come home sometimes with $60 or $50 a week. That was a lot of money, those days. And I worked there for about four years, three or four years. And then somebody opened up a delicatessen store, and he came to me, and I lent him money, $400. And his father-in-law was there, this fellow's father-in-law. And he said, [INAUDIBLE] he said, I would like to pay you interest.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said for me, you don't pay interest. I did you a favor. Sometimes I'll need a favor. You lend me the money. His father-in-law was there. His name was Mr. Smook. And Mr. Smook said, you know something? I have a friend of mine who has a daughter, and I would like you should come up and meet her. So I went to my cousin, I borrowed a coat, and I went up to see them. And that's how I met my wife. See, sometimes it's good to lend money for delicatessen stores. And then he took me up, and then, you know, we met-- we got married in 1950. We got married in October, 1950.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then after a while, since I was very handy, my father-in-law bought me a little rooming house and I ripped out the plumbing and I changed the lights and I fixed it up. And-- and I started, you know-- I knew-- I know every trade in the building business. Then after a while, I started to buy old buildings and remodel them and make new-- you know, remodel them to-- into residentials. And then I started to build new ones. And this is how I got myself into the real estate business. I became involved in real estate. I became involved in various civic undertakings in politics, in Jewish affairs, in Zionist affairs. You name it and I got it. You see all those-- over there you have all those plaques, you know, rewards. And I have three times as much downstairs too, you know, for various organizations. I became involved. And I have three children now. Sometimes I thought they already could be better, but I-- I am very happy. They are nice. And what's even more important, the grandchildren. I have four grandchildren. Very nice. Beautiful. And looking back, I could say [NON-ENGLISH] didn't do so bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you to continue with that just for a minute. 50 years ago, you were nowhere and you had nothing. You were alone, pretty much, in the world. And probably you weren't in very good shape physically either. You got married, you had a family, you became successful, you support a Holocaust center. You've been generous to the institutions.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well I-- I established a Holocaust center, which is called the Rosenthal Institute for Holocaust Studies, which is part of the City University New York. That was about three years ago, which I'm very proud of. The director is Randolph Braham. He's the director. Well-known man in the Holocaust, in particular the Hungarian Holocaust. And I am the-- what do you call it-- the benefactor or whatever. You know, I support it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did this happen? How do you account for this? How did you get from where you were 50 years ago to where you are today, and what do you make of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: If I would tell you I did it because I'm smart, don't believe it. Because sometimes people ask me a question, how do you just survive? I survived because I was lucky. It wasn't because I planned to survive. Listen, let me tell you something. When you are marching down a column and somebody comes and says, listen, half the column stay over here and half of the column stay over there, and those people who stay over here are machine-gunned, and I don't care how brave you are and how smart you are. How I survived was sheer luck.

speaker: interviewer
text: How I did this? Yes, hard work. I'll tell you, when I worked-- when I started to work here, even when I got vacation-- I remember when I worked in the factory there in Triangle Steel, when I got vacations-- two weeks a year, I got a vacation-- I went across the street. There was a lumber yard. And I got a job at the lumber yard.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, it was luck. And of course it was also, of course, you had to have a little brains to know what you're doing. And it's a combination of things. It's really a combination of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would I take the credit for all for myself? Of course a lot of it had to do with my family too. My wife and my father-in-law, you know, they helped me out. I don't want to take the credit, because it was a combination of things. I'm lucky, I guess. OK. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is a collage which was put together maybe about 20 years ago. And this here is my village where I came from, Comlaus. As you can see, here are the children sitting in the front. Because when they came home from cheder there was a family who came to America, so they took a picture. They took out those three-- those three boys from here, and I am in the middle. Take a good shot of my shoes. Those are the Gucci Gucci shoes. Original. Very seldom we wore shoes, especially in the summertime. We didn't have shoes in my village. But so-- you can see all the children, no shoes. But this is the village. This here is my youngest brother, Avrum, and this is my sister, Baile. This here is my grandmother. And this is my grandmother's mother, and this is my grandmother's father. Have I mentioned to you--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm going to repeat it.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, it's good to hear.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. As I say, this is my grandmother's mother, Edith. My grandmother's father, Shloimo Leazar. He died in 1942, because they took away all the fields from him. The Nazis took away the fields. He couldn't take it, so he died. My great-grandmother, she came with me to Auschwitz. This here is a star which we had to wear during the war. This is not my star. This is just a sample of a star which we had to wear during the war before-- before they put us into the ghettos, for the purpose of identification. Here this is-- in Buchenwald, they used to have their own currency. And this is a-- for the SS, Standarten Buchenwald. This is two Reichsmark. For this you could buy maybe a bottle of good water. I don't know. But it was-- the currency was only good in Buchenwald. This is a magazine which was printed after the war by a German-- by a German company about Buchenwald. And here, this is me standing up here on this side to your right. That's me after the war. This was maybe about a week or two after-- after liberation. There were a lot of movie cameras and photographers and paper-- newspapers in Buchenwald. This here is in Bucharest. This was taken in Bucharest about three months after the war. And you can still see on my very right, on the-- my lips on the right-hand side. This was a burn mark from the Nazis when they put in a match in Buchenwald to test whether I am still alive. I told you the story before. This was given to us by the American Army. This is the liberation paper. This is proof that we were in Buchenwald. After I went back to my village and I didn't find nobody alive, I came back and I settled in a DP camp. This camp-- I was in many DP camps. This camp was called Camp Herzog in [NON-ENGLISH]. This is a camp, Herzog in [NON-ENGLISH], which is near castle. I was there in that DP camp. From there I moved into a town which is called [NON-ENGLISH], near Frankfurt. This is another document from there. Here is-- after the war, I was looking for family, for various-- and I got responses that they couldn't find them. This is one response. That's another response. Here, I joined a--

speaker: interviewer
text: A little slower, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here-- in those pictures here, I joined the [NON-ENGLISH], which is a-- was an underground from the [NON-ENGLISH] to smuggle out-- in those days, it was illegal immigration to Palestine. And you could see me here dressed in the British uniform. And also here up front center. As I told you before, I joined a-- a Zionist group in Bergen-Belsen, which was called [NON-ENGLISH]-- [HEBREW]. And we went out on trainings and-- and stuff like this. Here I'm front row center. Here a member of the [HEBREW]. This is me dressed like in-- in British uniform. And also here. I'm in this picture too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one are you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here. After--

speaker: interviewer
text: And this one right up here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where?

speaker: interviewer
text: Number 12.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. This is just a group of-- of-- what happened is, there was one time in June 1946. June 1946. We were demonstrating against the British, and we broke the-- and we broke the gates and we stole the commander's gun. And there were riots over there and some fires and so on. And here there is a sign reading [GERMAN]-- 'we are not going to be locked up in camps anymore.' This was-- this year was more-- see, in Bergen-Belsen, there was-- from the Jewish brigade. Soldiers from the Jewish Brigade who were part of the British Army. And they told us many times what to do, instigate us and so forth. Because in those days there was a struggle for the Jewish homeland. So this was a very good source of, so to speak, propaganda.

speaker: interviewer
text: It just so happened, whoever stole the gun from the commander of Bergen-Belsen, he was the commander who liberated the camp. So he came back to us. He said, listen, this gun liberated you. I want to have back the gun. We gave him-- somebody gave it back to him. How should I know?

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyway-- see, because over there, we were constantly involved in-- in various undertakings. This here, embarkation on the Ernie Pyle. I was in-- in Bremerhaven in Germany. And I came to America, and after I had to report to the-- there was-- as I said, we were known as European war orphans, and I had to report once a week to-- they want to make sure I'm not a juvenile delinquent. Anyway, here I got a letter from them that I'm doing very-- very good. I adjusted to this-- to America, and I'm released from their-- from their obligation. And from there on I was all on my own. And I value this letter, because I did it on my own. I never took any-- any help from nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just one moment. When you finish that and get to the next picture, why don't you talk about the date and how old you were. The date's on the letter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the date is February, 1949. And this was an organization, which was I think in the '80s someplace. Actually, the headquarters was in the Hotel Marseilles on 103rd St. and Broadway. It's not there anymore. And you know, they want to know how we are doing and whether, you know, to make sure that we're going to adjust. And as I say, after a few weeks, I was released. As you could see in this picture-- this was also taken about the same time-- here I look like a Yankee. This is already in America when I was here already almost two years. Then I moved out to this area, and I became involved in building a synagogue to rebuild a temple. I've seen too many synagogues destroyed, so I started-- and here this is me. And this is the president. And this is-- today he's my partner, Abe Magid. And here we started to break grounds for the-- for the Shelter Rock Jewish Center. And then once I took my rabbi back to my village and to Auschwitz. So here you could see a picture as we enter Auschwitz. This is the gate to Auschwitz. Arbeit macht Frei. And this is the gate to Birkenau. And this here is by the monument in Birkenau. By the way, here when the temple was finished, you could see this is a picture of me. It says here Jack Rosenthal, President. I became the boss. The third line down there, it says Jack Rosenthal, President. And as I mentioned, those are all pictures when I went back with the rabbi, Rabbi [? Fenster ?] from Shelter Rock Jewish Center. We went back to Auschwitz. And from Auschwitz we went back to my village, which is called [NON-ENGLISH]. And you can see here the highway leading into the town. That's I-95. OK, and here we are meeting the mayor of Comlaus. This middle guy here. His name is [INAUDIBLE]. He was the mayor. And as you can see, he's still wearing the Gucci Gucci shoes. This is the mayor of Comlaus. And here he offers us a drink. And here those are the townspeople there. I mean the people from the village, where I tell them stories. And this is my family going back-- I don't know. This goes back quite a bit, maybe about 15, 18 years ago. And of course, my family takes most of the credit. And anyway, this is just a collage which I put together.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]. Don't go anywhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] No, the Auschwitz number I have on my hand, but in Buchenwald--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hold on a second.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's here. It's got to be here. Yeah, here it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lean it forward. Lean the whole thing forward.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: There you go. Read it, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: The number in Buchenwald-- in Auschwitz I had a number which was printed in my arm, A11832. In Buchenwald, my number was 120659. This could be 126659 or 120659. This is the number in Buchenwald. And as a matter of fact, they have the-- if anybody was going to Buchenwald, then give them this number. They will take out all my files from there. The Nazis kept good files. I also have-- in Auschwitz, in the archives in Auschwitz, my number is there too, in the museum. All right. Since my whole family cannot be here tonight, you are seeing my daughter Susan at her wedding date. She's married to Dr. Michael Katz. They live in California near San Jose, and they have two children. They are very beautiful children. One is a boy, and a girl. And Emily is the girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to see their pictures in a second, is that right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, we're going to see her. Just those two.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my daughter Barbara. She's here. You're going to meet her later. This is her husband, Martin Birnbaum. Fine young man. And he's home right now. He's home with my grandson. They live in [? New ?] City. Well, if somebody would ask me if it was worth it just to see those four children there, this is worth everything, because those are my four grandchildren. To your left, you see Emily. She's a Californian. Next to her is her brother, Benjamin.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, next to her in back is another sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. Next to her is the sister, Sarah. You're going to meet her later. She's here. And in front of Sarah is Benjamin, and Benjamin is my youngest grandchild. And next to him is Joshy, Joshua.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Mr. Rosenthal, please introduce the members of your family who could be here tonight.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is my wife, Elizabeth.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we are married how many years? Almost 45 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my daughter Barbara, my first born. This is Steven, my son. Then I have another, Susan. She's in California. And this is my oldest grandchild, Sarah. Sarah Emily Birnbaum. She's beautiful. BARBARA (DAUGHTER): I just wanted to say that being a child of a Holocaust survivor has been a burden but also a tremendous privilege, and that I'm very proud to be a child of a Holocaust survivor. And also, dad, I just want to say that you've set a tremendous example for us, not only in your courage and your determination, but in the kind of person you are and the kind of life you lead.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're a very charitable person, and we've learned what it means to live a good life and a meaningful life from you. And I just wanted to tell you that we love you very much and we know that you love us. How about ending with a [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: You want a [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SINGING] [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: BARBARA (DAUGHTER): The Rosenthal Family Singers.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's great.
